message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To estimate the effectiveness of 2-dose and 3-dose mRNA vaccination (BNT162b2 and mRNA-1273) against general SARS-CoV-2 infection (asymptomatic or symptomatic) caused by the omicron variant.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Propensity-score matched retrospective Cohort Study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Large public university undergoing weekly Covid-19 testing in South Carolina, USA.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Population consists of 24,145 university students and employees undergoing weekly Covid-19 testing between January 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    and January 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    , 2022. The analytic sample was constructed via propensity score matching on vaccination status: Unvaccinated, completion of 2-dose mRNA series within previous 5 months, and receipt of mRNA booster dose within previous 5 months. The resulting analytic sample consists of 1,944 university students and 658 university employees.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Intervention</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Vaccination with a two dose or 3 dose regimen of the BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273 vaccine.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Booster protection against any SARS-CoV-2 infection was 66.4% among employees (95% CI: 46.1-79.0%;
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <.001) and 45.4% among students (95% CI: 30.0-57.4%;
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <.001). Compared to the 2-dose mRNA series, estimated increase in protection from the booster dose was 40.8% among employees (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =.024) and 37.7% among students (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =.001). We did not have enough evidence to conclude a statistically significant protective effect of the 2-dose mRNA vaccination series, nor did we have enough evidence to conclude that protection waned in the 5-month period after receipt of the 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    or 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    mRNA dose. Furthermore, we did not find evidence that protection varied by manufacturer.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Covid-19 mRNA booster doses offer moderate protection against any SARS-CoV-2 infection caused by the omicron variant and provide a substantial increase in protection relative to the 2-dose mRNA vaccination series.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Covid-19 vaccine effectiveness against general SARS-CoV-2 infection from the omicron variant: A retrospective cohort study
